question: Compare Figure 5.4-7 (DHRS Schematic) with Figures 5.4-8 through 5.4-16 (performance curves). How do the curves validate the schematic design?

nuscale_ch05_0020.title: Steam Generators
nuscale_ch05_0020.summary: The steam generators (SGs) in the NuScale Power Module are integrated within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which also houses other key components such as the pressurizer and safety valves. The SG system includes feedwater and main steam piping, thermal relief valves, and access ports, with the RPV serving as the SG shell and forming part of the reactor coolant pressure boundary.

nuscale_ch05_0022.title: Decay Heat Removal System
nuscale_ch05_0022.summary: The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature designed to remove residual and core decay heat from the reactor after shutdown, especially when normal cooling is unavailable, without relying on external power. It ensures the reactor coolant system temperature drops below 420°F within 36 hours, maintaining fuel and pressure boundary safety limits, and its reliability and risk significance are rigorously evaluated. The DHRS operates independently of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS), but both can function together if needed.

nuscale_ch05_0000.title: Preface
nuscale_ch05_0000.summary: The Preface outlines the copyright and usage restrictions for the NuScale Standard Plant Design Certification Application, specifically Chapter Five on the Reactor Coolant System and Connecting Systems. It clarifies that the document is proprietary to NuScale Power LLC, with limited rights of use and reproduction granted to the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC) for regulatory purposes, and specifies requirements for handling both proprietary and nonproprietary versions.

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the NuScale pressurizer, including the RPV upper shell, baffle plate, heater bundles, and associated components. It specifies the types of steel and nickel-based alloys used, their protective claddings, and relevant material standards to ensure corrosion resistance and compatibility with operating environments. References to applicable sections and material specifications from the BPVC are also provided.

nuscale_ch05_0019.title: Reactor Coolant System Component and Subsystem Design
nuscale_ch05_0019.summary: This section describes the design of the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) components and subsystems in the NuScale Power Module, detailing the integration of the reactor, steam generators, pressurizer, safety and ECCS valves, and associated piping within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV). It also outlines the configuration and boundaries of the steam generator system, emphasizing the integral design of the steam generators within the RPV and the separation of primary and secondary sides by specific components.

nuscale_ch05_0023.title: Reactor Coolant System High-Point Vents
nuscale_ch05_0023.summary: This section describes the testing and inspection requirements for the DHRS (Decay Heat Removal System) components in the NuScale reactor, ensuring compliance with ASME codes for Class 2 components. It details how actuation valves are exercised and tested, including during cold shutdown conditions, and outlines the initial in-situ test to verify the DHRS function in the first reactor module.

nuscale_ch05_0009.title: Overpressure Protection
nuscale_ch05_0009.summary: This section outlines NuScale's compliance with overpressure protection requirements by referencing applicable ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) and Operations and Maintenance (OM) code cases, as mandated by various sections of 10 CFR 50.55a and related Regulatory Guides (RGs). It details how code cases for design, fabrication, construction, preservice, and inservice inspections and testing are selected and verified to meet NRC requirements, with a consolidated list provided in Table 5.2-1.

nuscale_ch05_0016.title: Pressure-Temperature Limits, Pressurized Thermal Shock, and Charpy Upper-Shelf Energy Data and Analyses
nuscale_ch05_0016.summary: This section outlines the basis for establishing operational pressure and temperature limits for the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) to comply with regulatory requirements over the plant’s 60-year lifespan. It describes the use of ASME and NRC methodologies to calculate pressure-temperature limit curves, accounting for vessel embrittlement, and provides figures and tables with these limits to ensure protection against non-ductile failure.

nuscale_ch05_0012.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Leakage Detection
nuscale_ch05_0012.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) leakage detection system does not use traditional components with designed leakage rates, making it impractical to distinguish between identified and unidentified leakage inside containment using installed instrumentation. All leakage into containment is treated as unidentified until the source is determined, with leakage rates monitored through RCS inventory balance and containment evacuation system (CES) flow rates, ensuring reliable detection to support safety analyses.

edges.description: Section A describes the inclusion and configuration of the DHRS and steam generators within the reactor coolant system, while Section B specifies the functional requirements and performance criteria that the DHRS must meet, directly linking the system's design (A) to its regulatory performance basis (B).

Section A provides a general description of the reactor coolant system components, including the pressurizer, while Section B specifies the detailed materials and construction of the pressurizer components referenced in Section A.

Section A is the preface providing copyright and usage information for the NuScale Design Certification Application, while Section B is a technical reference detailing specific reactor coolant system components and their materials; thus, Section A establishes the document's legal context, which is a prerequisite for accessing and using the technical content in Section B.

Section A provides the overall schematic and component arrangement of the DHRS, while Section B specifies the inclusion and function of the steam generators within that schematic, connecting the general system design to a specific subsystem.

Section A describes the overall reactor coolant system and identifies the DHRS as a subsystem, while Section B provides the specific design basis and functional requirements for the DHRS, connecting the general system overview to the detailed performance criteria.

Section A provides detailed technical descriptions of the DHRS and other reactor coolant system components referenced in the schematic (Figure 5.4-7), while Section B is a preface that introduces the overall document and its copyright, without technical content.

Section A specifies inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, including steam piping and valves, which are part of the broader system described in Section B that includes the steam generators and DHRS within the reactor pressure vessel.

Section A specifies the inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, which are part of the system whose design basis and safety function are described in Section B, thereby providing implementation details for the general system described in Section B.

Section A describes the overall design and components of the DHRS as part of the reactor coolant system, while Section B specifies the inspection and testing requirements that must be met for DHRS components to ensure their functionality as depicted in the schematic.

Section A outlines regulatory compliance and code requirements for overpressure protection, which are directly relevant to the design and operation of steam generators described in Section B, as both must adhere to the same ASME and NRC standards for reactor coolant system integrity.

Section A outlines compliance with ASME and NRC regulatory requirements for reactor coolant boundary integrity, while Section B describes the design basis of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS), which must adhere to these same codes and standards to ensure safe operation and regulatory compliance.

Section A discusses ASME code compliance for nonstructural attachments to the reactor pressure vessel, while Section B describes the components contained within the reactor pressure vessel, including steam generators and the decay heat removal system, connecting them through their shared physical and regulatory context within the reactor vessel design.

Section A discusses pressure-temperature limits and material requirements for the reactor vessel, while Section B outlines the design basis and operational function of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS); both sections are connected through the need for the DHRS to operate within the reactor vessel's structural and thermal limits to ensure safe shutdown.

Section B describes the overall design and components of the reactor coolant system, including the DHRS and steam generators, while Section A discusses the challenges of detecting leakage within this system, indicating that the integrity and configuration described in B directly impact the leakage detection limitations outlined in A.

Section A discusses the challenges of detecting reactor coolant pressure boundary leakage due to the absence of traditional components, while Section B describes the Decay Heat Removal System's design to safely remove decay heat from the reactor coolant system, with both sections connected by their focus on maintaining reactor coolant system integrity and safety under abnormal conditions.

Section A discusses compliance with ASME BPVC code cases for overpressure protection, while Section B details specific ASME BPVC requirements for reactor vessel attachments and welds, connecting them through their reliance on ASME BPVC standards for design and construction.

Section A outlines the regulatory codes and standards for overpressure protection and integrity of the reactor coolant boundary, while Section B discusses the detection of leakage from the reactor coolant pressure boundary, both addressing different aspects of maintaining RCPB integrity as required by regulatory compliance.

Section A discusses the structural and examination requirements for nonstructural attachments to the reactor pressure vessel, while Section B addresses the detection of leakage from the reactor coolant pressure boundary, both of which are connected through their focus on maintaining the integrity and monitoring of the reactor coolant pressure boundary as required by ASME BPVC standards.
